text.title: Rails ActiveRecord Failure Modes
text.summary: 5 antipatterns observed in real Rails ActiveRecord codebases. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Admin endpoint never shows deleted users; investigation reveals `default_scope { where(deleted_at: nil) }` on `User`.
antipattern.root-cause: `default_scope` applies to every call site without visible markers.
antipattern.fix: Replace with named scope (`scope :active, -> { where(deleted_at: nil) }`); force callers to opt in.

antipattern.symptom: `UserQuery` has 32 chainable methods; new developers can't tell which combinations are intended.
antipattern.root-cause: One Query Object accumulating every one-off filter.
antipattern.fix: Cap Query Objects at ~10 methods. Split by use case (`Users::ActiveQuery`, `Users::AdminSearchQuery`).

antipattern.symptom: Endpoint executes 200 queries; profile shows the extra SELECTs inside a `.html.erb` partial.
antipattern.fix: Move eager-load to the controller boundary. Add a `bullet`/`prosopite` gate to fail the test that triggers the regression.

antipattern.symptom: Background worker OOMs while exporting 2M users; process dies after 6 GB RSS.
antipattern.fix: Switch to `find_each` (default batch 1000) or `in_batches(of: 5000)`. For very wide rows, project to only needed columns first.

antipattern.symptom: Query Object encapsulates tenant filter but caller writes `User.where(id: ids)` inline elsewhere, missing the tenant scope.
antipattern.root-cause: Multiple entry points to the same data without a single source of truth.
antipattern.fix: Enforce: either every read goes through the Query Object OR every Query Object simply layers on top of model scopes (recommended). Add an RSpec helper asserting tenant scope.
